name: Valdepeñas and Villanueva de los Infantes Loop
name_original: Torre de Juan Abad desde Almuradiel – circular por Viso del Marqués y Valdepeñas
description: This difficult 80.0-mile road cycling route from Almuradiel offers a challenging ride through the Sierra Morena region.
region_intro: Road cycling around Almuradiel, located in Ciudad Real, offers diverse terrain within the Sierra Morena mountain range and near Despeñaperros Natural Park. The region features winding roads through Mediterranean forests, dramatic gorges, and extensive olive groves. Cyclists can expect varied elevation changes, from challenging ascents to more gentle stretches, providing a range of experiences. Best road cycling routes around Almuradiel The most popular road cycling route is Fresnedas River Gorges and Collado Pisa Loop, a 50.1 miles (80.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 42 minutes to complete. This difficult route navigates through scenic river gorges and offers significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Almuradiel - Viso del Marqu, a moderate 43.7 miles (70.3 km) path. This route connects two towns, providing a balanced ride through the local landscape. Local road cyclists also love the Castellar de Santiago from Almuradiel, a 51.0 miles (82.1 km) trail leading through the surrounding countryside, often completed in about 4 hours 4 minutes. Road cycling around Almuradiel is defined by Mediterranean forests, dramatic gorges, and extensive olive groves. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging routes with substantial climbs. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Almuradiel's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Almuradiel?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes available around Almuradiel, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. These routes navigate through the diverse landscapes of the Sierra Morena mountain range and near Despeñaperros Natural Park.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Almuradiel?
A: Road cycling around Almuradiel features diverse terrain, including winding roads through Mediterranean forests, dramatic gorges, and extensive olive groves. You'll encounter varied elevation changes, from challenging ascents to more gentle stretches, characteristic of the Sierra Morena and Despeñaperros Natural Park.

Q: What is the difficulty level of road cycling routes around Almuradiel?
A: The routes around Almuradiel cater to various fitness levels. You can find 1 easy route, 3 moderate routes, and 5 difficult routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1246865552/from-viso-del-marques-to-castillo-de-mudela-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Viso del Marqués to Castillo de Mudela — circular&lt;/a&gt; is an easy option, while routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1273156311/fresnedas-river-gorges-and-collado-pisa-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fresnedas River Gorges and Collado Pisa Loop&lt;/a&gt; offer significant climbs for experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there challenging routes with significant elevation gain for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, Almuradiel offers several challenging routes with substantial elevation gain. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1092434874/castellar-de-santiago-from-almuradiel" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castellar de Santiago from Almuradiel&lt;/a&gt; route features over 1,000 meters of ascent, providing a demanding ride through the surrounding countryside. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1273156311/fresnedas-river-gorges-and-collado-pisa-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fresnedas River Gorges and Collado Pisa Loop&lt;/a&gt; also includes nearly 1,000 meters of climbing.

Q: Can I find road cycling routes within Despeñaperros Natural Park?
A: Yes, Almuradiel's proximity to Despeñaperros Natural Park means many road cycling routes either pass through or offer stunning views of the park. The park is known for its dramatic gorges and panoramic viewpoints, making it a scenic area for road cyclists. Routes in this area often feature challenging ascents and descents.

Q: Are there scenic viewpoints accessible by road bike in the Despeñaperros Natural Park?
A: Absolutely. Despeñaperros Natural Park is home to several impressive viewpoints that are accessible by road bike. You can cycle to spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5620563" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Balcón de Despeñaperros Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5418434" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Desfiladero Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4215341" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador de los Órganos&lt;/a&gt;, offering breathtaking vistas of the gorges and surrounding landscapes.

Q: What natural landmarks or attractions can I discover while road cycling near Almuradiel?
A: Beyond the dramatic gorges of Despeñaperros, you can explore natural attractions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5418382" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cimbarra Mill&lt;/a&gt;, which is near the scenic Cimbarra waterfall. The region is also characterized by extensive olive groves, providing a pleasant backdrop for many rides.

Q: Are there historical sites or cultural attractions accessible by road bike in the Almuradiel area?
A: Yes, you can incorporate historical sites into your cycling routes. Notable attractions include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4072358" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Empedraillo&lt;/a&gt;, a short stretch of ancient Roman road, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4215375" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of Castillo de Castro Ferral&lt;/a&gt;. These offer a glimpse into the region's rich past while enjoying your ride.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the Almuradiel area?
A: Yes, there are circular road cycling routes available. An example is the easy &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1246865552/from-viso-del-marques-to-castillo-de-mudela-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Viso del Marqués to Castillo de Mudela — circular&lt;/a&gt; route, which offers a pleasant loop for cyclists. Many other routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Almuradiel?
A: The Sierra Morena region, where Almuradiel is located, experiences a Mediterranean climate. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter. These seasons provide ideal weather for exploring the winding roads and natural parks.

Q: Where can I park my car when starting a road cycling route in Almuradiel?
A: While specific parking areas for each route are not detailed, Almuradiel, as a small town, typically offers street parking or designated public parking spaces. For routes venturing into Despeñaperros Natural Park, visitor centers or trailheads within the park might provide parking facilities. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

Q: Are there any cafes or restaurants along the road cycling routes in Almuradiel?
A: Almuradiel and nearby towns like Viso del Marqués, which are connected by routes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1085915474/almuradiel-viso-del-marqu" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almuradiel - Viso del Marqu&lt;/a&gt;, offer local cafes and restaurants where you can refuel. The region's small villages often have establishments serving traditional Spanish cuisine, perfect for a mid-ride break.
tips:
In addition to the Palace of the Marqués de Santa Cruz, which is a Renaissance jewel built between 1564 and 1586 by the Spanish sailor Álvaro de Bazán, Viso del Marqués is home to other places of interest:


Church of Our Lady of the Assumption: This Gothic-Renaissance style church has a central nave offset from the presbytery, built on the remains of a previous church. On its walls, you will find the curious “Lagarto del Viso”, a stuffed crocodile brought from the Nile by the Marquis during one of his expeditions.


Plaza del Pradillo: The center of the town is surrounded by the Marqués Palace, the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption and the Tower of the old convent.


AVAN Natural Sciences Museum: An interesting place to explore nature and biodiversity.


Hermitage of San Andrés and forest park of the Valle de los Perales: A natural environment where you can enjoy the beauty of the Sierra de Viso del Marqués


Viso del Marqués is a destination full of history and charm! 🏰🌿

In it is the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption, occupies one of the flanks of the Plaza Mayor of the town. In plan it has two Herrerian-influenced naves: the main one and San Lorenzo, which is shorter, and a Mannerist chapel on the gospel side.
In the main nave is the altarpiece, a copy of the one from 1553, destroyed in the Civil War, of which six panels have been preserved that correspond to a pre-Mannerist style prior to Correa de Vivar, which some hypotheses attribute to the Hernando Yánez School ( disciple of Raphael). At the end of this nave, the choir on a pointed arch.
In the nave of San Lorenzo, a work by Gregorio Prieto with the figure of the patron saint, the Virgin of Consolation.
The Mari Sánchez reservoir or as it is known, La Cabezuela collects the waters of the Jabalón river. Water levels vary dramatically with the seasons.

The Parish Church of Our Lady of the Elms, in Torre de Juan Abad (Ciudad Real), is one of the most emblematic churches in Campo de Montiel, with great historical and artistic value.
It was built on the site of a small existing chapel in 1243, a work of the Order of Santiago, whose cross-sword can still be seen in the pediments.
Declared a Site of Cultural Interest (BIC) in 2005.

This charming place is located in the south of the province of Ciudad Real, near Andalusia, and is part of the Sierra Morena region of Ciudad Real. 74 kilometres south of the provincial capital, Almuradiel is crossed by the Southern Highway and is located between kilometre points 231 and 243. Near the town, the Despeñaperros Pass begins, a gorge carved by the Despeñaperros River.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a hard road ride, covering 129 kilometers with 510 meters of elevation gain. You'll find predominantly well-paved surfaces, including 121 km of asphalt and 8.01 km of paved roads. Be aware of short sections (around 206 meters) of cobblestones, and some segments might even require you to dismount and push your bike.

faqs.question: Is this racebike route suitable for beginner cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and is a 'Hard road ride,' demanding a very good level of fitness. It's best suited for experienced cyclists due to its distance, elevation, and some challenging terrain sections.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for this 129-kilometer racebike tour is just over five hours, specifically around 5 hours and 8 minutes, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: The tour starts from Almuradiel. You should be able to find parking within the town. For specific parking recommendations, it's advisable to check local maps or community forums for Almuradiel.

faqs.question: What historical and natural sights can I expect to see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a mix of cultural and natural highlights. You'll pass through Valdepeñas and near the Embalse de Mari Sánchez. In Torre de Juan Abad, look for the Parish Church of Our Lady of the Elms, a significant Gothic-Renaissance landmark. Viso del Marqués features the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption, the impressive Marqués Palace, and the AVAN Natural Sciences Museum. You'll also encounter the San Bernardo Convent and the Church of the Immaculate Conception of Almuradiel.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Sierra Morena region can experience hot summers. For a more comfortable ride, consider cycling during spring or autumn when temperatures are milder. Always check the weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route that primarily uses public roads. While there aren't specific restrictions against dogs on the route itself, bringing a dog on a 129 km racebike tour is generally not practical or safe for the animal. If you plan to visit towns or specific attractions, check their individual pet policies.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route, as it primarily uses public roads. However, if you plan to visit specific historical sites or museums along the way, they may have their own admission fees.

faqs.question: Does this route pass through any official trails or parks, such as Campo de Montiel?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route traverses the Sierra Morena region, which includes parts of the historical Campo de Montiel area. While it's mainly a road cycling route, it briefly intersects with very small sections of official trails like Ruta de los Molinos (1%), Cañada Conquense o De Los Serranos (1%), and Camino de Almuradiel (2%). There are no specific entrance fees or permits required for cycling these small segments.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay overnight near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through several towns including Almuradiel, Viso del Marqués, and Valdepeñas, you will find various options for cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. Valdepeñas, being a larger town, will offer the most choices.

faqs.question: Are there public transport options to reach the starting point in Almuradiel?
faqs.answer: Almuradiel is bisected by the Southern Highway, suggesting it's accessible. While direct public transport for cyclists with bikes can sometimes be limited in rural Spain, bus services often connect larger towns. It's best to check specific bus schedules and policies regarding bicycle transport from major cities like Ciudad Real or Valdepeñas to Almuradiel.
